speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been having these palpitations off and on since last Monday—February 13th. They usually happen in the afternoon and last a few minutes.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Can you tell me more about the palpitations? Any dizziness, shortness of breath, or chest discomfort with them?

speaker: patient
text: No chest pain or trouble breathing, but sometimes I feel a little lightheaded right after. And my hands get a bit shaky.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your vitals now—I’m getting your heart rate at 62 bpm. That’s within normal range, though it’s on the lower side.

speaker: patient
text: Really? I thought it might be racing when I feel those flutters.

speaker: doctor
text: Your body temperature is 37.3 °C—completely normal. No sign of fever or infection today.

speaker: doctor
text: Blood pressure is 132 over 80 mmHg—diastolic is 80, which is fine, but we’ll keep an eye on it given your symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been managing my blood sugar pretty closely lately. I’m on Metformin 1000mg twice a day.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, I see that in your chart—you’re currently on Metformin 1000mg bid. That aligns with your diagnosis of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, which we confirmed last year and reaffirmed today based on your HbA1c and fasting glucose.

speaker: doctor
text: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus remains your primary diagnosis. We’ll continue monitoring your glycemic control closely.

speaker: patient
text: Do you think the palpitations could be related to my diabetes—or maybe something else like stress or caffeine?

speaker: doctor
text: It’s possible—but we also need to rule out arrhythmias, thyroid dysfunction, or electrolyte imbalances. Let’s order an ECG and thyroid panel next week.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, that makes sense. I’ve also noticed my energy levels are lower than usual this week.

speaker: doctor
text: Any recent changes in sleep, diet, or medications? Any new over-the-counter supplements?

speaker: patient
text: No new meds or supplements. Just the same routine—though I did skip my morning Metformin once because I felt nauseous.

speaker: doctor
text: Interesting—I just rechecked your temperature, and it’s 36.0 °C. That’s slightly lower than the 37.3 °C I recorded earlier, so it may reflect a mild diurnal variation or measurement difference.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your borderline diastolic pressure and recent lightheadedness, I’m going to add Amlodipine 5mg once daily to help with vascular tone and reduce cardiac workload.

speaker: patient
text: You mentioned my heart rate was 62 bpm earlier—that reading matches what I saw on my wrist monitor this morning, so it seems consistent.

speaker: doctor
text: Good to hear. We’ll repeat the BP and HR in two weeks, and I’d like you to log any palpitation episodes—time, duration, triggers, and how you felt before and after.

speaker: patient
text: Will do. Thanks, Doctor—I’ll start Amlodipine tomorrow and bring my log to the follow-up.
